subject: Physics
q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest through the same potential difference. The ratio of linear momenta acquired by above two particals will be :
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest through the same potential difference. The ratio of linear momenta acquired by above two particals will be :
solution:
For α particle, mα=4 a.m.u and charge qα=2e.

For proton, mp=1 a.m.u and charge qp=e.

As the charged particle are moving through same potential, the kinetic energy acquired by the particle will be equal to electric potential energy i.e., 12mv2=qV, where, V is potential.

Using the relation between momentum p and kinetic energy, we have 

p=2mE=2mqV

Then, the ratio of linear momenta of two particles is pαpp=mαqαmpqp=41×21

=221=22:1

subject: Physics
q: A point source of light is placed at the centre of curvature of a hemispherical surface. The source emits a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The radius of curvature of hemisphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and the inner surface is completely reflecting. The force on the hemisphere due to the light falling on it is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
question: A point source of light is placed at the centre of curvature of a hemispherical surface. The source emits a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The radius of curvature of hemisphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and the inner surface is completely reflecting. The force on the hemisphere due to the light falling on it is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
solution:
For completely reflecting surface, force, F=2IAc, where, I=PSurface area is intensity of radiation, A is surface area and c is speed of light.

F=2IcπR2=2P04πR2πR2c

=P02c=242×3×108=4×10-8 N

subject: Physics
q:
Monochromatic light of frequency 6×1014 Hz is produced by a laser. The power emitted is 2×10-3 W. How many photons per second on an average, are emitted by the source?

(Given h=6.63×10-34 J s)
question:
Monochromatic light of frequency 6×1014 Hz is produced by a laser. The power emitted is 2×10-3 W. How many photons per second on an average, are emitted by the source?

(Given h=6.63×10-34 J s)
solution:
Energy of a single photon is given by, hν. Therefore, power can be written as 

P=nhv

⇒n=Phν=2×10-36.63×10-34×6×1014

=5×1015

subject: Physics
q: Electron beam used in an electron microscope, when accelerated by a voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> has a de-Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the voltage is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron beam would be:
question: Electron beam used in an electron microscope, when accelerated by a voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> has a de-Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the voltage is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron beam would be:
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V is 

λ=hmv=h2 m(KE)=h2 meV

where, h is Planck's constant, e is charge of electron and m is mass of electron.

From above relation, λ∝1V

∴ λλ0=2040

⇒ λ=λ02.

subject: Physics
q: Two particles move at right angle to each other. Their de Broglie wavelengths are <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively. The particles suffer perfectly inelastic collision. The de Broglie wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> of the final particle, is given by:
question: Two particles move at right angle to each other. Their de Broglie wavelengths are <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively. The particles suffer perfectly inelastic collision. The de Broglie wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> of the final particle, is given by:
solution:
P1→=hλ1i^,    P2→=hλ2j^
P→=P1→+P2→=hλ1i^+hλ2j^
P→=h1λ12+1λ22
hλ=h1λ12+1λ22
1λ2=1λ12+1λ22

subject: Physics
q:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: If a strong diffraction peak is observed when electrons are incident at an angle 'i' from the normal to the crystal planes with distance ' $d$ ' between them (see figure), de Broglie wavelength $\lambda_{\mathrm{dB}}$ of electrons can be calculated by the relationship ( $n$ is an integer)
question:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: If a strong diffraction peak is observed when electrons are incident at an angle 'i' from the normal to the crystal planes with distance ' $d$ ' between them (see figure), de Broglie wavelength $\lambda_{\mathrm{dB}}$ of electrons can be calculated by the relationship ( $n$ is an integer)
solution: $2 d \cos i=n \lambda_{d B}$

q: Which of the following statement is not true about stopping potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right)$ ?

It increases with increase in intensity of the incident light.

It depends upon frequency of the incident light.
solution: $\mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} \nu-\phi_0=\mathrm{eV}$

question: A particle is moving <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> times as fast as an electron. solution:
λ=hmv
⇒  m'm=vλv'λ'=15×11.878×10-4×9.1×10-31
m'=9.7×10-28kg

question: Photon of frequency $\mathrm{v}$ has a momentum associated with it. If $\mathrm{c}$ is the velocity of light, the momentum is

Statement II : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.

In the light of the above statements choose the correct answer from the option given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : Davisson-Germer experiment establishes the wave nature of electrons.

Statement II : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.

In the light of the above statements choose the correct answer from the option given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution: In Davisson–Germer experiment, the high speed electrons are incident on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ni</mi></math> crystal and they get diffracted just like a wave. So wave nature of electron was experimentally observed in this experiment.

q: Two stream of photons, possessing energies equal to twice and ten times the work function of metal are incident on the metal surface successively. The value of ratio of maximum velocities of the photoelectrons emitted in the two respective cases is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: Two stream of photons, possessing energies equal to twice and ten times the work function of metal are incident on the metal surface successively. The value of ratio of maximum velocities of the photoelectrons emitted in the two respective cases is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
KEmax=hν-ϕ

12mv2=hν-ϕ

v=2hν-ϕm

Given hν1=2ϕ

hν2=10ϕ

∴ v1v2=hν1-ϕhν2-ϕ

v1v2=2ϕ-ϕ10ϕ-ϕ=13

q: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
question: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{h} \nu-\phi \\ & 0.5 \mathrm{~V}=2.48-\phi \\ & \text { work function }(\phi)=2.48 \mathrm{~V}-0.5 \mathrm{~V}=1.98 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

If both the particles move along the <math><mtext>x</mtext></math> -axis after the collision, the change <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>λ</mi></math> in the wavelength of the particle <math><mtext>A</mtext></math>, in terms of its de-Broglie wavelength <math><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></math> before the collision is:
question: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>m</mtext><mtext>A</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>m</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> moving along the <math><mtext>x</mtext></math> -axis with velocity <math><mrow><msub><mtext>v</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></math> collides elastically with another particle <math><mtext>B</mtext></math> at rest having mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>m</mtext><mtext>B</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>m</mtext><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. If both the particles move along the <math><mtext>x</mtext></math> -axis after the collision, the change <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>λ</mi></math> in the wavelength of the particle <math><mtext>A</mtext></math>, in terms of its de-Broglie wavelength <math><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></math> before the collision is:
solution:
v1=2m/30m2+m3+m2−m3vm2+m3=v5

For particle A,

Initial de-Broglie wavelength

λ0=hm2v=2hmv

Final de-Broglie wavelength after collision.

λ1=hm2v5=10hmv=5λ0

Change in De-Broglie wavelength Δλ=λi−λ0=4λ0

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Two photons having equal linear momenta have equal wavelengths.

Statement II: If the wavelength of the photon is decreased, then the momentum and energy of a photon will also decrease.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Two photons having equal linear momenta have equal wavelengths.

Statement II: If the wavelength of the photon is decreased, then the momentum and energy of a photon will also decrease.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
solution:
If linear momentum are equal then wavelength also equal.

The momentum p and energy E of a photon is given by,

p=hλ, E=hcλ; where c is speed of light and h is Plank's constant.

On decreasing wavelength, momentum and energy of photon increases.

q: A point source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> emits light with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency. At a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the source, the intensity produced by the electric field component is:
question: A point source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> emits light with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency. At a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the source, the intensity produced by the electric field component is:
solution:
Total power emitted =100×5100=5 W

Now intensity due to electric field will be half of the total intensity. Therefore,

IE=12×powerarea=12×54π×52

=140πWm2

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
options: Half of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Four times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
<math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></math>  of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Two times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is given as

λ=hp

∵p22m=k⇒p=2mk

⇒λ=h2mk

At ground level energy =E0 so

At n=4 level energy =E0n2=E016

∴ at ground level

λ0=h2m E0

At n = 4 level

λ′=h2mE016=4h2m E0

⇒λ′=4λ0

q: An electron, a doubly ionized helium ion ($He^{++}$) and proton are having the same kinetic energy. The relation between their respective de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>He</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> is :
question: An electron, a doubly ionized helium ion ($He^{++}$) and proton are having the same kinetic energy. The relation between their respective de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>He</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> is :
solution:
$\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m k}}$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}: \lambda_{\mathrm{p}}: \lambda_{{He}^{++}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{He}^{++}}}$
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-27}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}}=84: 2: 1$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A): The electromagnetic wave exerts pressure on the surface on which they are allowed to fall.
Reason (R): There is no mass associated with the electromagnetic waves.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A): The electromagnetic wave exerts pressure on the surface on which they are allowed to fall.
Reason (R): There is no mass associated with the electromagnetic waves.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Assertion (A) is true because electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum. When they strike a surface, they transfer momentum to it, which results in a force and consequently exerts radiation pressure on the surface.

Reason (R) is true because electromagnetic waves consist of photons, which have zero rest mass. Thus, there is no rest mass associated with electromagnetic waves.

However, the pressure exerted by electromagnetic waves is due to the momentum they carry ($p = \dfrac{E}{c}$), not because they are massless. Therefore, both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

Answer: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest splits into two parts having masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo><</mo><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The ratio of de Broglie wavelength of two parts will be
question: A nucleus of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest splits into two parts having masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>M</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo><</mo><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The ratio of de Broglie wavelength of two parts will be
solution:
Since the initial momentum of the system is zero. To keep the final momentum zero, the momentum of both parts should be equal and opposite in direction.

P→1=P→2

Here P→ is momentum

De-Broglie wavelength is given by λ=hP

Hence, both will have same de Broglie wavelength.

Therefore, λ1:λ2=1:1.

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is $\lambda_e$ and the de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton accelerated through the same potential difference is $\lambda_p$. If their corresponding masses are $m_e$ and $m_p$, respectively, then the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths $\left(\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}\right)$ is ______.
question: The de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is $\lambda_e$ and the de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton accelerated through the same potential difference is $\lambda_p$. If their corresponding masses are $m_e$ and $m_p$, respectively, then the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths $\left(\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}\right)$ is ______.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of a particle accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}$.

For an electron, the charge is $e$ and mass is $m_e$. Its de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}$.

For a proton, the charge is $e$ and mass is $m_p$. Its de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_p = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_p eV}}$.

Taking the ratio of the two wavelengths, we get $\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p} = \dfrac{\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}}{\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_p eV}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m_p}{m_e}}$.

Answer: $\sqrt{\dfrac{m_p}{m_e}}$

subject: Physics
q: A parallel beam of light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mi>Wm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is incident on a surface perpendicular to the beam. The number of photons crossing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> area perpendicular to the beam in one second is
question: A parallel beam of light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mi>Wm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is incident on a surface perpendicular to the beam. The number of photons crossing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> area perpendicular to the beam in one second is
solution:
Wavelength of incident beam λ=900×10-9 m

Energy of a single photon E=hcλ.

Intensity is defined as power per unit area. Therefore,

Intensity of incident beam I=EtA

If the number of photons crossing the given area per second is n, then

n=Enet Esingle pholon =IAλhc

⇒n=1001×10-4900×10-96.62×10-34×3×108=4.5×1016.

question: An electron (of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> ) and a photon have the same energy <math><mi>E</mi></math> in the range of a few <math><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron and the wavelength of the photon is ( <math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo></math> speed of light in vacuum)
solution:
λd for electron =h2mE
λ for photon =hCE
Ratio =h2mEEhC=1CE2m

q: The work functions of cesium (Cs) and lithium (Li) metals are 1.9 eV and 2.5 eV , respectively. If we incident a light of wavelength 550 nm on these two metal surfaces, then photo-electric effect is possible for the case of
question: The work functions of cesium (Cs) and lithium (Li) metals are 1.9 eV and 2.5 eV , respectively. If we incident a light of wavelength 550 nm on these two metal surfaces, then photo-electric effect is possible for the case of
solution:
\(E=\frac{1240 \mathrm{eV} \cdot \mathrm{~nm}}{550 \mathrm{~nm}}=2.25 \mathrm{eV}\)
Possible for \(C_s\) as \(E > \phi_{c s}\)
Not for Li as \(E < \phi_{L i}\)

subject: Physics
q: $K_1$ and $K_2$ be the maximum kinetic energies of photoelectrons emitted from a surface of a given material for the light of wavelength $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, respectively. If $\lambda_1=2\lambda_2$ then the work function of material is given by:
question: $K_1$ and $K_2$ be the maximum kinetic energies of photoelectrons emitted from a surface of a given material for the light of wavelength $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, respectively. If $\lambda_1=2\lambda_2$ then the work function of material is given by:
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_1} - \phi$

$K_2 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - \phi$

Given $\lambda_1 = 2\lambda_2$, the first equation becomes:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda_2} - \phi$

Multiplying by $2$:

$2K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - 2\phi$

Substituting $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} = K_2 + \phi$ from the second equation:

$2K_1 = (K_2 + \phi) - 2\phi$

$2K_1 = K_2 - \phi$

$\phi = K_2 - 2K_1$

Answer: $K_2-2K_1$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). If the incident frequency is now doubled, the photocurrent and the maximum kinetic energy are also doubled.
Statement 2: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from a surface is linearly dependent on the frequency of the incident light. The photocurrent depends only on the intensity of the incident light.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). If the incident frequency is now doubled, the photocurrent and the maximum kinetic energy are also doubled.
Statement 2: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from a surface is linearly dependent on the frequency of the incident light. The photocurrent depends only on the intensity of the incident light.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends upon frequency on incident light and photo current depends upon intensity of incident light.

q: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">P</mi></msub></math> are accelerated through the same potential difference. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron to that with the proton is
question: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">P</mi></msub></math> are accelerated through the same potential difference. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron to that with the proton is
solution:
KE=eΔV
λe=h2 me(eΔV)
λp=h2 mp(eΔV)

⇒  λeλp=mpme

subject: Physics
q:
The threshold wavelength for photoelectric emission from a material is 5500 A∘. Photoelectrons will be emitted, when this material is illuminated with monochromatic radiation from a

A. 75 W infra-red lamp

B. 10 W infra-red lamp

C. 75 W ultra-violet lamp

D. 10 W ultra-violet lamp

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The threshold wavelength for photoelectric emission from a material is 5500 A∘. Photoelectrons will be emitted, when this material is illuminated with monochromatic radiation from a

A. 75 W infra-red lamp

B. 10 W infra-red lamp

C. 75 W ultra-violet lamp

D. 10 W ultra-violet lamp

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Given here, threshold wavelength λth=5500 Å=550 nm

For photoelectric emission, the wavelength of the light should be less than 550 nm.

Wavelength of infrared radiation extends from 700 nm-1 mm and that of ultraviolet radiation is 100-400 nm.

Since, UV light has less wavelength than 550 nm, so only UV would be able to emit photoelectrons.

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength for an electron accelerated through the potential difference of $V_1$ volt is $\lambda_1$. When the potential difference is changed to $V_2$ volt, the associated de Broglie wavelength is increased by $50\%$. If $(V_1/V_2) = (9/\alpha)$, then the value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: The de Broglie wavelength for an electron accelerated through the potential difference of $V_1$ volt is $\lambda_1$. When the potential difference is changed to $V_2$ volt, the associated de Broglie wavelength is increased by $50\%$. If $(V_1/V_2) = (9/\alpha)$, then the value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$.

This implies $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$.

Given that the new wavelength $\lambda_2$ is increased by $50\%$, we have $\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 + 0.5\lambda_1 = \dfrac{3}{2}\lambda_1$.

Taking the ratio of the wavelengths, we get $\dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}}$.

Substituting $\lambda_2 = \dfrac{3}{2}\lambda_1$, we get $\dfrac{2}{3} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}}$.

Squaring both sides yields $\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{4}{9}$, which gives $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{9}{4}$.

Comparing this with the given relation $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{9}{\alpha}$, we find $\alpha = 4$.

Answer: $4$

subject: Physics
q: The energy $E$ and momentum $p$ of a moving body of mass $m$ are related by some equation. Given that c represents the speed of light, identify the correct equation
question: The energy $E$ and momentum $p$ of a moving body of mass $m$ are related by some equation. Given that c represents the speed of light, identify the correct equation
solution:
We need to check the dimensions only.
With momentum the dimension
$E^2=p^2 C^2$
And with mass $E^2=m^2 c^4$
So $E^2=p^2 C^2+m^2 c^4$ (dimensionally)
